title: 02 - Cell Injury, Cell Death, and Adaptations - Flashcards

chapter.title: Cell Injury, Cell Death, and Adaptations

description: Third-party generated Chapter 2 flashcards optimized for active recall, spaced repetition, and exam revision. The deck follows the Chapter 2 syllabus buckets and prioritizes high-yield mechanisms, distinctions, morphologic patterns, and aging concepts from Robbins Basic Pathology, 10th Edition, Chapter 2.

disclaimer.use_note: Use these flashcards as revision support alongside the original source material.

deck.deck_name: Pathology::Chapter 02 - Cell Injury, Cell Death, and Adaptations
topic_buckets: Types of cell injury and death
Adaptations of cellular growth and differentiation
deck.design_note: Cards are kept mostly single-fact and under one question per card for rapid recall.
deck.page_reference_note: The [n] citations in card backs and the source_page_refs arrays use the printed book page number from Robbins Basic Pathology 10th Edition, not the sequential PDF page position. Chapter 2 occupies book pages 31-56.

cards.topic: Most common cause of hypoxic injury
cards.front: What is the most common cause of clinically important hypoxic injury?
cards.back: Ischemia due to reduced blood supply, most often from arterial obstruction. [32]

cards.front: How does ischemia differ from simple hypoxia?
cards.back: Ischemia also reduces nutrient delivery and causes toxic metabolite buildup, not just oxygen deficiency. [32][42]

cards.front: Which category of cell injury includes autoimmunity and allergy?
cards.back: Immunologic reactions. They often injure host tissues through inflammation. [32]

cards.front: What two broad factors commonly interact to cause diseases such as hypertension, diabetes, and cancer?
cards.back: Inherited genetic susceptibility plus environmental triggers. [31]

cards.front: What kind of toxin requires metabolic activation before becoming injurious?
cards.back: A latent toxin, often activated by cytochrome P-450 in smooth ER. [45]

cards.front: Which environmental gas can cause hypoxic injury by reducing the oxygen-carrying capacity of blood?
Which broad cause of cell injury includes trauma, temperature extremes, radiation, electric shock, and pressure change?
What term describes a reversible new steady state that lets a cell survive stress?
What is the most common morphologic correlate of reversible cell injury?
What lipid-related morphologic change is the other main correlate of reversible injury?
What term is used for vacuolar reversible injury caused by swelling of ER-derived spaces?
cards.back: Hydropic change, also called vacuolar degeneration. [33]

cards.front: What plasma membrane feature commonly appears in reversible cell injury?
cards.back: Membrane blebbing with blunting or loss of microvilli. [33]

cards.front: What single functional failure most strongly marks irreversible cell injury?
cards.back: Inability to restore mitochondrial oxidative phosphorylation. [34]

cards.front: What is the usual sequence of necrotic nuclear changes?
What pump failure causes early cell swelling in hypoxic injury?
cards.back: Failure of ATP-dependent sodium pumps, often described as Na+/K+ ATPase failure. [42]

cards.front: What metabolic pathway increases when oxidative phosphorylation fails?
What acid accumulates during prolonged ATP depletion?
What paradoxical process can worsen tissue damage after blood flow returns to an ischemic organ?
Which reactive oxygen species is formed from hydrogen peroxide in the presence of iron?
Which enzyme converts superoxide to hydrogen peroxide?
What is the main initial goal of the unfolded protein response?
cards.back: To reduce the load of misfolded proteins in the ER so the cell can restore protein homeostasis. [45][46]

cards.bucket: Types of cell injury and death
cards.front: What type of cell death is always pathologic and typically inflammatory?

cards.bucket: Types of cell injury and death
cards.front: What type of cell death clears cells without significant inflammation?

cards.bucket: Types of cell injury and death
cards.front: Which necrosis pattern characterizes solid-organ infarcts outside the brain?

cards.bucket: Types of cell injury and death
cards.front: Which necrosis pattern is typical of bacterial abscesses and brain infarcts?

cards.bucket: Types of cell injury and death
cards.front: Which necrosis pattern is classically associated with tuberculosis?

cards.bucket: Types of cell injury and death
cards.front: Which kinases are central to necroptosis?

cards.bucket: Types of cell injury and death
cards.front: What is the main purpose of autophagy during starvation?
cards.back: Recycling cellular components to provide nutrients and energy. [40][41]

cards.bucket: Adaptations of cellular growth and differentiation
cards.front: What does hypertrophy mean?

cards.bucket: Adaptations of cellular growth and differentiation
cards.front: What does hyperplasia mean?

cards.bucket: Adaptations of cellular growth and differentiation
cards.front: What is the classic pathologic cardiac example of hypertrophy?
cards.back: Left ventricular hypertrophy caused by hypertension or aortic valve disease. [48][49]

cards.bucket: Adaptations of cellular growth and differentiation
cards.front: What is the classic example of compensatory hyperplasia?

cards.bucket: Adaptations of cellular growth and differentiation
cards.front: What does atrophy mean?
cards.back: Shrinkage of cells due to loss of cell substance. [50]

cards.bucket: Adaptations of cellular growth and differentiation
cards.front: Which pathway mediates much of the protein breakdown in atrophy?

cards.bucket: Adaptations of cellular growth and differentiation
cards.front: What epithelial change occurs in the bronchi of smokers?
cards.back: Ciliated columnar epithelium is replaced by stratified squamous epithelium. [50][51]

cards.front: What is steatosis?
cards.back: Accumulation of triglycerides within parenchymal cells. [51]

cards.front: Which organ is most commonly affected by steatosis?
What do hyaline droplets in proximal tubules usually represent?
Which common disease can cause abnormal intracellular glycogen accumulation?
What is anthracosis?
cards.back: Carbon pigment accumulation in the lungs and draining tracheobronchial lymph nodes. [52]

cards.front: What is lipofuscin?
cards.back: A wear-and-tear pigment formed by free-radical peroxidation of lipids and proteins. [52]

cards.front: Which stain identifies hemosiderin?
What is dystrophic calcification?
cards.back: Calcium deposition in injured or dead tissue despite normal serum calcium levels. [53]

cards.front: What is metastatic calcification?
cards.back: Calcium deposition in normal tissues because of hypercalcemia. [53][54]

cards.front: Which valve lesion is a classic example of dystrophic calcification?
cards.back: Calcific aortic stenosis of an aging or damaged aortic valve. [53]

cards.front: What endocrine disturbance most classically causes metastatic calcification?
cards.back: Excess parathyroid hormone, or PTHrP acting similarly in malignancy. [53]

cards.front: Where does dystrophic calcification often begin ultrastructurally?
cards.back: In membrane-bound vesicles or in the mitochondria of dying cells. [53]

cards.front: Which organs are commonly affected by metastatic calcification?
cards.back: Kidneys, lungs, gastric mucosa, and systemic vasculature. [54]

cards.front: What term describes massive calcium deposition in the kidney?
What are telomeres?
cards.back: Short repeated DNA sequences at chromosome ends that protect chromosome integrity and support complete replication. [54][55]

cards.front: What causes replicative senescence in most somatic cells?
Which enzyme maintains telomere length?
Why do many cancer cells reactivate telomerase?
cards.back: To stabilize telomeres and permit indefinite proliferation. [55]

cards.front: What does defective protein homeostasis cause in aging cells?
cards.back: Loss of normal proteins together with accumulation of misfolded proteins. [55]

cards.front: How does calorie restriction affect aging pathways?
cards.back: It slows aging-associated pathways, partly by reducing insulin/IGF signaling and improving repair and protein homeostasis. [55][56]

cards.front: What chronic process accelerates aging and promotes diseases such as atherosclerosis and type 2 diabetes?
